retrieved_docs.title: Mental Nerve Block -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mental foramen is most commonly located halfway between the alveolar crest and the inferior border of the mandible in direct line with the second premolar or between the two premolars [5] . However, it is hard to determine its exact position since it varies for every individual. On the horizontal plane, the foramen can localize mesial, distal, or between the apices of the premolar roots. On the vertical plane, it can be found above, at, or below the apices of the premolars. The variations are influenced by gender, age, race, and technique used for assessment (ie, palpation, Panorex, computed tomography scan). Furthermore, the alveolar bone resorption, typical of edentulous patients, makes the location of the mental foramen closer to the alveolar crest [6] . Despite its variability, multiple studies have demonstrated complete anesthesia when localizing the foramen with palpation and landmarks.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mental Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The orifice of exit of the mental nerve, mental foramen , is most commonly located halfway between the alveolar crest and the inferior border of the mandibular bone in the vertical plane [6] and, in the horizontal plane, at the level of the apex of the second premolar or between the premolars [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mental Nerve -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: As the trigeminal nerve exits the pons, it will divide into the ophthalmic nerve, the maxillary nerve, and the mandibular nerve. The mandibular nerve leaves the skull through the foramen ovale to give rise to a meningeal nerve called the nervus spinosus, which re-enters the skull through the foramen spinosum, and a nerve for the medial pterygoid muscle [2] . After this, the main trunk of the mandibular nerve further divides into an anterior and posterior branch. Next, the posterior trunk splits into three branches: the auriculotemporal, lingual, and the inferior alveolar nerve. The inferior alveolar nerve enters the mandible through the mandibular foramen and travels throughout the mandibular canal anteriorly until reaching the mental foramen. It receives the name of the mental nerve once it exits this foramen [3] [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mental Nerve -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The branching of the mental nerve may vary slightly. After exiting the mental foramen, it usually splits into three branches, but there may be more or fewer divisions in some individuals. More than one mental foramen may occur in around 1% of patients as a result of the mandibular canal bifurcation in the inferior-superior plane or medial-lateral plane [8] [9] . However, the sensory territory is consistent [10] [11] .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The shape of the mandibular foramen may vary depending on its contents. The mandibular foramen's location, thickness, and length are dependent on the inferior alveolar nerve and artery. [2] [3] The shape of the orifice of the mandibular foramen can be circular or even V-shaped in some individuals. The mandibular foramen vary depending on the age of the person also. The opening of the mandibular foramen is generally more superior in the ramus of the mandible in adults. In contrast, the mandibular foramen is located lower in the mandible in children. [4] In some cases, the mandibular foramen was found to have an extra canal. The extra foramen is called the accessory mandibular foramen. This extra foramen is present in individuals with bifid inferior alveolar nerves. The location of the accessory mandibular foramen is inferior to the primary mandibular foramen. In rarer variations, there are three mental foramina as the endpoint of the mandibular foramen. [5] [6] These variations can be found and confirmed on radiographic imaging. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Mental Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: The IAN becomes the mental nerve as it leaves the mental foramen and is found to branch either into 1, 2, or 3 branches at, or immediately after, it exits the mental foramen. [12] Before exiting the mental foramen, the IAN may pass beyond this foramen and create a loop in an anteroinferior direction of variable distance. [13] The mental foramen lies between the 2 mandibular premolars in 50.4% to 61.95% of cases and apical to the 2nd premolar in 50.3% to 57.9% of cases. [14] Careful consideration of the mental nerve and the anterior loop of the IAN with appropriate imaging is critical when planning the extraction of an impacted mandibular tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Mental Nerve Block -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (third branch) leaves the cranium through the foramen ovale . Immediately after, it gives rise to two nerves, a nerve to the medial pterygoid muscle and nervus spinosus. The trunk of the mandibular nerve then splits into two branches, anterior and posterior. The anterior branch mostly gives motor innervation, and the posterior branch, mainly sensory, has 3 divisions: auriculotemporal, inferior alveolar, and lingual nerve. The inferior alveolar nerve enters the mandible through the mandibular foramen, traverses the mandibular canal, and exits through the mental foramen, receiving the name of the mental nerve. [4] The incisive nerve continues in the mandibular canal to innervate the premolars, canines, and incisors [1] . See Image. Permanent Teeth, Viewed From the Right.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Once the mandibular foramen ends at the mental foramen, the inferior alveolar nerve and artery will become the mental nerve and artery. On the outer medial surface of the mandibular foramen, there is a groove called the mylohyoid fossa. This fossa is designed for the mylohyoid nerve (branch of the inferior alveolar nerve) as it travels toward the mylohyoid muscle. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Foramen -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The mandibular foramen is a bony canal formed within the mandible. This bony canal opens on the inner side of the mandibular ramus. The opening is directly inferior to the mandibular notch and ends at the mental foramen. The foramen follows the shape of the mandible. The canal starts inferior to the mandibular notch and descends until it's slightly superior to the angle of the mandible. At the point superior to the angle of the mandible, the canal will make a turn towards the anterior chin region. After the canal makes the turn, it will travel and end at the mental foramen, which is slightly lateral to the anterior chin region. The mandibular foramen contains a branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V), a branch of the maxillary artery and the inferior alveolar vein. The nerve and blood vessels within the mandibular foramen are the inferior alveolar nerve, artery, and vein. The inferior alveolar nerve is responsible for innervation of the gums, dental sockets, and mandible. The inferior alveolar artery is the dominant blood supply to the mandible, gums, teeth, and nerves in the lower jaw. The inferior alveolar vein is responsible for the venous drainage of the mandible and tissues that are nearby.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Middle Cranial Fossa -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The trigeminal nerve, CN V, is a mixed motor and sensory nerve that has a significant relationship to the middle cranial fossa as its ganglion and branches course through many middle cranial fossa structures. Of structural importance is the location of the trigeminal ganglion in the intracranial cavity within Meckel’s cave. Meckel’s cave is a mouth shaped depression in the middle cranial fossa, lateral to the cavernous sinus, that is continuous with the posterior cranial fossa and contains the trigeminal nerve, trigeminal ganglion, along with V1, V2, and V3 (mandibular) branches. [14] Stemming from Meckel’s cave, the V1 and V2 branches enter the cavernous sinus along the lateral sinus wall slightly inferior to the trochlear nerve. The branches of the trigeminal nerve then exit the middle cranial fossa through the superior orbital fissure (V1), foramen rotundum (V2), and foramen ovale (V3). [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Middle Cranial Fossa -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The structure and function of the middle cranial fossa center around the foramina that transmit a variety of cranial nerves and vessels. In the anterior midline of the middle fossa within the sphenoid bone, the optic foramina open into the optic canals which ultimately terminate into the orbital cavities. Of clinical and surgical importance, the prechiasmatic sulcus is a depressed groove between the optic foramina and plays a role in skull base surgery due to its predisposition to hiding cranial base meningiomas. Due to this reason, extensive research has been conducted to establish the anatomic and radiological morphology of the sulcus. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Structure and Function -- Body
retrieved_docs.content: The bilateral mental foramina are an important landmark on the external mandibular surface, as it allows access to the mental nerve for a local anesthetic block. This landmark lies halfway between the upper and lower borders of the mandibular body, most commonly on the imaginary line which would run between the first and second premolars, although this varies with age. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Foramen Spinosum -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The proximity of the foramen spinosum to the foramen ovale and trigeminal nerve provides it with significant clinical relevance. The foramen spinosum acts as an anatomical landmark during neurosurgical procedures. Some example procedures include surgeries to treat trigeminal neuralgia, tumors, and epilepsy. There have been various techniques used to treat trigeminal neuralgia such as microvascular decompression, stereotactic radiosurgery, and percutaneous procedures including radiofrequency rhizotomy or balloon decompression. The percutaneous procedures, in particular, require cannulation of the foramen ovale which may become difficult due to the potential anatomical variation of the shape of the foramen. The majority of times the foramen ovale is oval in shape, but in some individuals, it is almond, round or slit-shaped. Having an idea of the anatomical location of the foramen ovale can play an essential role in facilitating successful CT-guided cannulation percutaneously. One study of interest looked at 100 dry human skulls (70 male, 30 female), studying them for any anatomical variations of the foramen ovale. Researchers measured the distance between the foramen oval and foramen spinosum. According to the study, the mean distance between the foramen ovale and foramen spinosum in the male subjects was 4 mm on the right and 3.8 mm on the left side, and in the females, 3.6 mm on the right and 3.2 mm on the left side, respectively. This estimated distance between the foramen ovale and foramen spinosum may ease our ability to locate the foramen ovale for percutaneous cannulation and can increase the chances of success of the procedure. There are other studies that have examined which angle of cannulation the foramen ovale has. [1] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Frontal Bone -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The frontal bone contains 3 foramina: a paired set of supraorbital foramina and a singular foramen caecum. The bilateral supraorbital notches lie at the superior orbital rim, just medial to the midpupillary line, transmitting the supraorbital artery, veins, and nerves. [8] The foramen caecum appears on the internal surface near the posterior border, where the frontal bone meets the ethmoid bone, and carries an emissary vein to the superior sagittal sinus. This sinus follows a longitudinal groove running anteroposteriorly along the frontal bone. [9] The anteroinferior portion of the frontal bone houses 2 frontal sinuses located superior to the orbital roofs. These sinuses vary widely in size and volume between individuals. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Structure and Function -- Skull
retrieved_docs.content: The angle of the mandible is where the mandible body meets the ramus, forming a distinct corner or angle. The mandibular notch (sigmoid notch) is a depression between the condylar and coronoid processes on the ramus' superior border. The mandibular foramen on the ramus' internal surface transmits the inferior alveolar vessels and nerves, supplying the lower teeth and the mandible. The mandibular symphysis is where the 2 halves of the mandible fuse anteriorly at the midline. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandible -- Structure and Function -- Ramus
retrieved_docs.content: The internal surface of the ramus is punctured by the mandibular foramen, situated halfway between the anterior and posterior borders of the ramus at the level of the occlusal surfaces of the lower teeth, and through which the inferior alveolar nerve and vessels enter the mandibular canal, ultimately terminating at the mental foramen. [9] The lingula is a small flap of bone that overlies the anterior part of the mandibular foramen, to which the sphenomandibular ligament attaches. [10]  The mylohyoid groove is an inferior continuation from the lingula, through which the mylohyoid nerve and vessels travel after piercing the sphenomandibular ligament. See Image. Jaw Anatomy, Medial Aspect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 731
retrieved_docs.content: NORMAL DELIVERY, AND OTHER INDICATIONS FOR CARE IN PREGNANCY, LABOR, AND DELIVERY, indicating that understanding malpositions informs decisions about normal versus assisted delivery. Finally, Mentum presentation of fetus is identified as an isa relationship with مّجيء وَجْهي (face presentation), specifying that a mentum (chin) presentation is a particular form of facial fetal presentation. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how various fetal positions, malpresentations, and physiological reproductive events are organized and interpreted to guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 963
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is an instance of the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that this fetal presentation can occur regardless of the specific clinical episode. The “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” category is classified as a type of “Malposition; malpresentation,” placing it within the overall group of abnormal fetal positions. Within the same “Malposition; malpresentation” group, other specific abnormalities are also classified, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The specific entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is linked to the Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” confirming that it refers to a fetal arm that has prolapsed. Additionally, “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is an instance of the broader “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” further illustrating the hierarchical organization of these malpresentation categories. Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy in which face or brow presentations, prolapsed fetal arms, and transverse or oblique presentations are all subtypes of malposition or malpresentation, each linked to more general descriptive categories that are independent of specific episodes of care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that affect labor and delivery comprise a broad obstetric category that includes abnormal fetal presentations, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, cervical pathology, amniotic‑membrane and fluid disorders, fetal structural anomalies, and bone‑health considerations influencing obstetric management. Abnormal fetal presentations documented in this domain are breech malpresentation (often converted to cephalic), face or brow presentation (including mentum variant), transverse or oblique presentation, shoulder (arm) presentation, funic presentation, poor fetal presentation, unstable fetal position, fetal arm prolapse, and fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion; fetal disproportion may also result from myelomeningocele. These atypical positions frequently require operative vaginal delivery, special obstetric maneuvers, or cesarean section rather than routine spontaneous vaginal birth. Multiple‑gestation pregnancies are represented by twin, triplet, quadruplet,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that is linked hierarchically to the more general term “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” indicating that the former is a child concept of the latter. This generic face‑or‑brow presentation is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation, placing it within the broader category of abnormal fetal positions. Similarly, Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus are each connected to their respective generic descriptors (“Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable”) through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 834
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that encompasses a variety of specific fetal and obstetric entities, procedural concepts, and anatomical factors relevant to the process of childbirth. Within this category, the condition Breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation is listed, indicating a successful repositioning of a breech fetus to a head‑down orientation prior to delivery. The category also includes Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, a neural‑tube defect that can cause size mismatch between the fetus and the maternal pelvis, and this specific condition is situated within the Fetal period, highlighting its occurrence during fetal development. Several fetal abnormalities that affect labor are enumerated as child concepts of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY. These include سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition), استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal presentation entities “Face OR brow presentation of fetus” and “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” are each linked to more generic descriptors—“Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” respectively—indicating that the specific presentations are sub‑categories of broader, episode‑agnostic classifications. Both of these broader presentation categories are classified under the overarching obstetric condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which groups together abnormal fetal positions that deviate from the normal cephalic presentation. “Malposition; malpresentation” itself is identified as an indication for obstetric management within the domain “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” highlighting that any malpresentation warrants specific clinical attention during prenatal care, labor, and delivery planning. Within the “Malposition; malpresentation” group, two additional sub‑types are recognized: “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The prolapsed‑arm condition also has a parent term expressed in Arabic, “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” which serves as the linguistic equivalent of the English descriptor. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that specific fetal malpresentations—face or brow presentation, transverse or oblique presentation, and prolapsed fetal arm—are organized under the general category of malposition/malpresentation, which is a key indication for specialized obstetric care throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This structured classification aids clinicians in recognizing, documenting, and managing abnormal fetal positions to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 614
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is organized under the broader category “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” which in turn is classified as a type of malposition; malpresentation. This malposition; malpresentation category is a central classification within “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” indicating that fetal malpositions are important considerations in obstetric management. Malposition; malpresentation also classifies other specific fetal position abnormalities, including “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The entity “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” has the parent term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” the Arabic expression for a prolapsed fetal arm, while “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” is linked to its parent “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Additionally, the relationship “has parent” connects the concept of malposition; malpresentation with the Arabic phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين,” which translates to “instability of fetal position,” reinforcing that these malpresentations represent unstable fetal orientations. Collectively, these entities and their hierarchical relationships delineate how various fetal presentations—face or brow, prolapsed arm, transverse or oblique—are categorized under the overarching malposition; malpresentation framework, highlighting their relevance for clinical decision‑making during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: Face or brow presentation of fetus is a specific type of Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, and it is classified under the broader category Malposition; malpresentation. This relationship indicates that Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable has a parent connection to Malposition; malpresentation. Fetal presentation finding also has a broader relationship to Malposition; malpresentation, situating it within the same overarching classification of abnormal fetal positions. Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery likewise list Malposition; malpresentation as a parent, reflecting the clinical importance of recognizing these presentations when planning obstetric care. Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable is classified as a form of Malposition; malpresentation and has a parent relationship to the Arabic term تدلّي ذراع الجنين, linking the English and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s medical knowledge integrates obstetric, fetal, and pharmacologic domains. It details maternal‑fetal conditions that affect labor and delivery, emphasizing abnormal fetal presentations such as breech, face or brow (مّجيء وَجْهي), mentum, transverse (المَجيء المستعرض), oblique, shoulder (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), compound (samengesteld; ligging), Funic presentation, prolapsed arm (تدلّي ذراع الجنين), and unspecified malposition or malpresentation. These malpositions, described by Arabic terms سوء المجيء الجنيني, عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين, and the Czech “naléhání plodu”, are classified under the broader category malposition; malpresentation, which is a child of “Indications for care in pregnancy, labor, and delivery” and linked to the physiological phenomena of the reproductive system. Unstable fetal position is also recognized. Size‑and‑shape mismatches such as cephalopelvic disproportion, fetal myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, cerebral hydrocephalus‑induced

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1064
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus, also described as Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, is a specific type of fetal malposition that is classified under the broader category Malposition; malpresentation. This malposition is directly relevant to Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, which lists Malposition; malpresentation as its parent concept. Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable (Arabic: تدلّي ذراع الجنين) and Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus, with its corresponding unspecified presentation category (Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable), are likewise subtypes classified as Malposition; malpresentation, each having a parent relationship to their specific presentation descriptors. Unspecified malposition or malpresentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, is linked to the broader relationship of Malposition; malpresentation, reflecting the overall instability of fetal position (Arabic: عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Collectively, Malposition; malpresentation serves as the parent category that classifies these various fetal presentations—including Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, and Transverse or oblique presentation—providing a hierarchical framework that guides clinical assessment and management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: through a “has parent” relationship, showing that the specific fetal findings are sub‑categories of the unnamed generic forms. Both the prolapsed arm and the transverse/oblique presentations are also classified as malposition; malpresentation, indicating that they are particular manifestations of fetal malposition. The Arabic term “تدلّي ذراع الجنين” (prolapsed fetal arm) is identified as the parent label for the prolapsed arm presentation, while the phrase “عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين” (instability of fetal position) is associated with the “has parent” relationship for malposition; malpresentation, reinforcing the concept that malposition reflects an unstable fetal orientation. In addition, the broader concept “Fetal presentation finding” is linked to malposition; malpresentation through a “has a broader relationship” connection, underscoring that abnormal presentations such as face or brow, prolapsed arm, and transverse/oblique positions are all encompassed within the overall category
